title: Personal Pronouns (Accusative & Dative)
description: Learn how German personal pronouns change their form depending on their role in the sentence - whether they are the subject, direct object, or indirect object.

sections.title: 1. Understanding the Three Main Cases
sections.description: In German, a pronoun changes its form depending on its "job" in the sentence.

cases.description: The person or thing doing the action.
The person or thing receiving the action directly.
Usually the "receiver" of a gift or the person for whom something is done. It is also required after specific prepositions like "mit" (with) or "zu" (to).

sections.title: 2. Personal Pronouns in Accusative
sections.description: We use the Accusative when a person is the direct object of a verb. Common "Accusative Verbs" include lieben (to love), mögen (to like), kennen (to know a person), and sehen (to see).

examples.explanation: "Ich" is the subject (Nominative). "He" becomes "ihn" because he is being loved (Direct Object).
"Du" is the subject. "Me" becomes "mich" because I am the object of the liking.
"Wir" is doing the seeing. "You all" becomes "euch."

content.note: For the pronouns sie (she/they), es (it), and Sie (formal You), the form does not change between Nominative and Accusative.

sections.title: 3. Personal Pronouns in Dative
sections.description: The Dative case is used for the "beneficiary" of an action (the person getting something) or after specific verbs like helfen (to help), gefallen (to please/like), and antworten (to answer).

examples.explanation: The book is the direct object (Accusative), but "you" are the receiver, so "du" becomes dir.

examples.english: The dress pleases me (I like the dress).
examples.explanation: The verb "gefallen" always triggers the Dative case for the person who likes the object.
"Helfen" is a Dative verb. Even though she is the direct object in English, in German, she is Dative.

sections.title: 4. The Role of Prepositions
sections.description: Sometimes the case is decided not by the verb, but by the little word before the pronoun.

sections.title: 5. Summary of Key Transformations
sections.description: To help you remember these without a chart, think of them in groups:

groups.title: The "We" and "You plural" Pronouns
groups.note: wir (we) becomes uns in both Accusative and Dative. ihr (you all) becomes euch in both Accusative and Dative.

groups.title: The "They" and Formal "You" Pronouns
groups.note: sie/Sie stays sie/Sie in Accusative. sie/Sie becomes ihnen/Ihnen in Dative.

sections.title: 6. Practical Usage: "How are you?"
sections.description: This is a classic Dative expression. In German, you don't say "How are you?"; you say "How is it going to you?"

examples.note: Literally: It goes well to me
